literal: of Paśupati, "Lord of beasts/bound souls"; from paśu (bound soul) + pati (lord)
invariant_definition: Pāśupata is the oldest organized Śaiva ascetic tradition, traced to Lakulīśa (c. 2nd c. CE) and codified in the Pāśupata-Sūtra with Kauṇḍinya's commentary. Its central analysis is threefold: pati (the Lord, Śiva, as Paśupati), paśu (the bound soul, the "beast"), and pāśa (the bonds of karma and matter that fetter it). Liberation is the soul's attainment, by the Lord's grace and a graded discipline, of union with or proximity to Śiva and of Śiva-like powers, ending all suffering. It is theistic and dualistic in tendency, and its observances famously included deliberately transgressive ascetic practices designed to provoke censure and thereby transfer demerit. The pati-paśu-pāśa triad was inherited and reworked across later Śaiva schools.
